title: Neurologic Emergencies: Altered Mental Status & Seizures
learningObjectives: Apply the AVPU scale and PAT Appearance findings to recognize altered mental status in children.
List the common reversible causes of pediatric altered mental status using the AEIOU-TIPS framework.
Describe field management of active pediatric seizures, including status epilepticus.
Recognize febrile seizures as generally benign and distinguish them from more concerning seizure presentations.
Identify field priorities for a child with suspected increased intracranial pressure.

summary.title: Recognizing Altered Mental Status
summary.content: The PAT's Appearance leg is often the first clue to altered mental status, before a formal AVPU (Alert, Voice, Pain, Unresponsive) assessment is completed. Any child who is not appropriately alert and interactive for their developmental stage — or who has an abnormal TICLS finding such as poor eye contact or an abnormal cry — should prompt a full neurologic assessment. Serial reassessment matters: a trend toward decreasing responsiveness is more concerning than a single snapshot value.

summary.title: Reversible Causes: AEIOU-TIPS
summary.content: A useful framework for reversible causes of pediatric altered mental status is AEIOU-TIPS: Alcohol/substance use, Epilepsy/seizure (postictal state), Infection, Overdose/opioids, Uremia/metabolic, Trauma/temperature, Insulin/hypoglycemia, Psychiatric, Shock/stroke. Hypoglycemia deserves particular emphasis in children because it is common, rapidly reversible, and easily checked in the field with a glucose meter — every altered child should have a glucose check when available.

summary.title: Seizures: Field Management
summary.content: Most pediatric seizures are brief and self-limited. Field priorities during an active seizure are protecting the airway (positioning, suction as needed — do not put anything in the mouth), protecting the child from injury, checking glucose, and timing the seizure. A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, or repeated seizures without return to baseline between them, meets the definition of status epilepticus and is a time-critical emergency requiring rapid escalation of care and transport; benzodiazepine dosing for status epilepticus is detailed in the PALS reviewer.

summary.title: Febrile Seizures
summary.content: Febrile seizures are the most common pediatric seizure type, typically occurring in children 6 months to 5 years with a rapidly rising fever. A simple febrile seizure is generalized, lasts less than 15 minutes, and does not recur within 24 hours — these are generally benign, and the child typically returns to baseline mental status afterward. A complex febrile seizure (focal features, lasting more than 15 minutes, or recurring within 24 hours) or any seizure in a child who does not return to baseline warrants a higher level of concern and should not be assumed to be a simple febrile seizure.

summary.title: Suspected Increased Intracranial Pressure
summary.content: Signs suggesting increased intracranial pressure include a bulging fontanelle (infants), a decreasing level of consciousness, an irregular respiratory pattern, and — as a late and ominous finding — the Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations). Field priorities are avoiding hypoxia and hypotension (both worsen secondary brain injury), positioning to avoid jugular venous compression, and rapid transport; specific hyperventilation/hyperosmolar therapy parameters are beyond this chapter's field-recognition scope.

clinicalPearls: Every altered child gets a glucose check when available — hypoglycemia is common and rapidly reversible.
AEIOU-TIPS is a useful recall framework for reversible causes of altered mental status.
A seizure lasting more than 5 minutes, or repeated seizures without return to baseline, is status epilepticus — a time-critical emergency.
Simple febrile seizures are generalized, under 15 minutes, don't recur in 24 hours, and the child returns to baseline — anything more should raise concern.
redFlags: Any altered child without a glucose check performed when a meter is available.
Seizure activity lasting more than 5 minutes or recurring without return to baseline (status epilepticus).
Bulging fontanelle, irregular respirations, or the Cushing triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) suggesting increased intracranial pressure.
commonMistakes: Assuming all pediatric seizures with fever are simple febrile seizures without checking duration/recurrence/focality.
Delaying a glucose check in an altered child when a meter is available.
Placing an object in the mouth of a seizing child (unnecessary and can cause injury).
memoryAids: AEIOU-TIPS: reversible causes of altered mental status.
5 minutes = status epilepticus — the clock matters.
Simple febrile seizure: generalized, under 15 minutes, no recurrence in 24 hours, back to baseline after.
Cushing triad = late and bad: high BP, low HR, irregular breathing.

decisionTrees.title: Pediatric Altered Mental Status and Seizure Field Approach
decisionTrees.content:
Altered mental status noted (PAT Appearance abnormal / AVPU below Alert)
  → Check glucose when available → treat hypoglycemia per protocol if present
  → Consider AEIOU-TIPS causes: Alcohol/substance, Epilepsy/postictal, Infection, Overdose, Uremia/metabolic, Trauma/temperature, Insulin/hypoglycemia, Psychiatric, Shock/stroke
  → If actively seizing:
     Protect airway/position, protect from injury, time the seizure, check glucose
     <5 minutes and resolves, returns to baseline, fits simple febrile seizure pattern → supportive care, transport for evaluation
     ≥5 minutes OR repeated seizures without return to baseline → Status epilepticus → time-critical emergency, escalate immediately (see PALS reviewer for benzodiazepine dosing)
  → If signs of increased intracranial pressure (bulging fontanelle, irregular respirations, Cushing triad) → avoid hypoxia/hypotension, position to avoid jugular compression, rapid transport

references: AAP Pediatric Education for Prehospital Professionals (PEPP), 4th Edition — Neurologic Emergencies
courses/pals/data/pals — cross-referenced for status epilepticus drug dosing and advanced resuscitation depth
